questions.question: What is a care plan?
options: A list of a person's problems and diagnoses only
A legal contract the person signs before any care can begin
The written record of how a person wishes to be supported, what matters to them and the outcomes they hope to achieve
A fixed timetable of tasks that applies to every person a service supports

questions.question: Which piece of legislation underpins the assessment of a person's needs and the duty to produce a care and support plan in England?
options: The Care Act 2014
The Human Rights Act 1998
The Health and Safety at Work Act 1974
The Equality Act 2010

questions.question: Care planning is best described as which of the following?
options: A task carried out only by family members
An ongoing cycle of assessment, plan and review
A single document completed once and then filed away
A form the care worker writes from scratch each visit

questions.question: Who is responsible for carrying out the formal assessment and authoring the care plan?
options: The care worker delivering daily support
A trained assessor or senior colleague
Any family member who is present
The person's neighbour or friend

questions.question: In the care planning cycle, what is the purpose of a review?
options: A regular check that the plan is still right and still working
To assess a person's eligibility for the first time
To remove the person from the service
To write the very first version of the plan

questions.question: What does person-centred care mean?
options: Doing tasks as quickly as possible to fit more people in
Planning support around the individual's own life, preferences and goals
Letting the care worker decide what is best because they are trained
Fitting the person into a fixed routine so care is consistent

questions.question: The Care Act 2014 is built on the idea of wellbeing. Which of these is part of that wellbeing?
options: The number of workers assigned to a person
The speed at which tasks are completed
A person's dignity, control over daily life and relationships
The cost of the support provided

questions.question: What is the difference between a task and an outcome?
options: A task is something a worker does, while an outcome is what the person gains
A task is what the person gains and an outcome is what the worker does
An outcome is always written by the person and a task by the family
There is no difference; the words mean the same thing

questions.question: Which of these is written as an outcome rather than a task?
options: To be able to get to the community lunch club and stay in touch with friends
Assist the person to wash and dress each morning
Empty and clean the commode after each use
Prompt the person to take their medication at 8am

questions.question: What is a strengths-based approach to care planning?
options: Planning that builds on the abilities, resources and relationships a person already has
Planning that ignores what a person can do to save time
Planning that focuses only on a person's problems and needs
Planning led entirely by the person's family

questions.question: If a person can butter their own toast but needs help making the tea, what should the plan do?
options: Leave out the detail because it is trivial
Ask the family to make both for them
Record that they manage the toast, so no one takes over what they can do
Say a worker should do everything to be safe

questions.question: What does the Care Act 2014 require regarding the person's role in their own plan?
options: The person must be involved in preparing their plan and have a genuine say
Only professionals may contribute to the plan
The person may only sign the plan once it is finished
The plan should be written for the person without their input

questions.question: Where someone would struggle to be involved in their plan, who can help their voice be heard?
options: A member of the public
An independent advocate
The quickest available worker
The Care Quality Commission inspector

questions.question: How should family knowledge be used in a care plan?
options: Only if the family member is a nurse
It should always override what the person wants
With the person's consent, to make the plan richer, while the person's voice comes first
Instead of asking the person anything

questions.question: Which law sets out how to support people to make their own decisions and what to do when someone may not be able to?
options: The Care Act 2014
The Data Protection Act 2018
The Equality Act 2010
The Mental Capacity Act 2005

questions.question: What is the starting assumption under the Mental Capacity Act 2005?
options: That the worker decides what is safest
That family should decide for the person
That a person cannot make decisions until proven otherwise
That a person can make their own decisions

questions.question: A person with capacity makes a choice about their care that you think is unwise but they clearly understand it. What does the Mental Capacity Act expect?
options: Assume they lack capacity because the choice seems unwise
Respect their decision, as a person with capacity may make an unwise choice
Ask a relative to make the decision instead
Overrule them because your job is to keep them safe

questions.question: When a person genuinely cannot make a particular decision even with support, any decision made on their behalf must be:
options: In their best interests and the least restrictive option
Whatever the family prefers
Decided by the care worker alone
The cheapest option available

questions.question: Capacity under the Mental Capacity Act is described as:
options: Only relevant to people over 65
The same for every decision a person faces
Fixed for life once assessed
Decision-specific, so a person may manage some decisions and need help with others

questions.question: What does the SMART approach to outcomes stand for?
options: Safe, monitored, assessed, reviewed, timed
Specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-bound
Simple, manageable, agreed, recorded, tidy
Strengths, motivation, ability, resources, teamwork

questions.question: What does positive risk-taking mean in a care plan?
options: Weighing a risk against the benefit to the person's independence, choice and quality of life
Removing every risk by removing the activity
Letting the family decide all risks
Ignoring risks so the person can do what they like

questions.question: Once a care plan is agreed, what is the care worker's central duty?
options: To ignore the plan and use their own judgement
To rewrite the plan as they see fit
To work to the plan so everyone supports the person in the same agreed way
To share the plan publicly with others

questions.question: You arrive to find a task in the care plan no longer seems to suit the person. What should you do?
options: Skip the task altogether because you disagree with it
Quietly do the task a different way and say nothing
Follow the agreed plan for now and report your concern so it can be reviewed
Wait until the annual review months away before mentioning it

questions.question: Which of these is an example of offering everyday choice while following the plan?
options: Asking which jumper they would like, or tea or coffee
Doing tasks in whatever order is quickest
Telling the person what they must wear
Deciding the person's whole day for them

questions.question: Over several visits you notice a person needs much more help than usual to walk to the bathroom. What is the best action?
options: Record what you observed factually and report it so the plan and risks can be reviewed
Decide it is just old age and not worth mentioning
Say nothing unless the person falls
Change the person's mobility support yourself without telling anyone

questions.question: A care plan is a living document that is never finished and changes as the person does.
options: True
False

questions.question: A care worker is responsible for carrying out the formal assessment and writing the care plan.
options: True
False

questions.question: A person should be actively involved in preparing and reviewing their own care and support plan.
options: True
False

questions.question: Making a choice that others see as unwise, on its own, proves that a person lacks capacity.
options: True
False

questions.question: Consent can be withdrawn at any moment, and the care worker must respect that.
options: True
False

questions.question: A care plan should be reviewed only once, when it is first written.
options: True
False

questions.question: Care records must be kept confidential and handled in line with the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018.
options: True
False

questions.question: Escalating a change in a person's needs means going over your colleagues' heads and is discouraged.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are stages of the care planning cycle? (Select all that apply)
options: Discharge from all services
Prosecution
Assessment
Review
Care and support plan

questions.question: What should a good, person-centred care plan capture? (Select all that apply)
options: Only the person's medical diagnoses
The person's strengths
The person's wishes and outcomes
The person's needs
Only the tasks that are quickest for the worker

questions.question: Which are key principles of the Mental Capacity Act 2005? (Select all that apply)
options: Assume a person can make their own decisions
A relative can always take over any decision for the person
Support the person to make the decision before concluding they cannot
A person has the right to make what others see as an unwise choice

questions.question: Which of these are types of change you might notice and should record and report? (Select all that apply)
options: The colour of the walls in the person's home
A change in the person's health, such as new pain or poor appetite
A change in mood or situation, such as low spirits or a carer becoming unwell
A change in ability, such as a task they now struggle with

questions.question: Which of the following describe a good care record? (Select all that apply)
options: Accurate and factual, recording what you saw and did
Written from memory at the end of the week
Timely, written as soon as you can
Kept confidential and stored securely
Full of your personal opinions about the person and their family

questions.question: How can you support good teamwork and communication around a care plan? (Select all that apply)
options: Keep useful observations to yourself to save time
Pass on important information clearly at handovers
Ask a senior when you are unsure rather than guessing
Write in the person's records so the next worker knows what happened

questions.question: Which situations may trigger a care plan review sooner than planned? (Select all that apply)
options: After a spell in hospital
When nothing about the person has changed at all
After a fall
After a bereavement

questions.question: A care plan is a ___ document that is reviewed regularly.
Person-centred care means planning support around the ___, treating them as the expert on their own life.
The Mental Capacity Act 2005 requires that any decision made on a person's behalf is in their best ___ and is the least restrictive option.
A SMART outcome is specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-___.
When a change affects a person's safety or wellbeing and cannot wait for the next review, you need to ___ it promptly to the right person.
